title: 3.0:1 ANSI-black/bright-black/selection contrast failures can never block doctor/fix/mode (severity hard-coded to WARN by threshold)
description: _check_pair sets severity via `FAIL if threshold >= 4.5 else WARN` (diagnostics.py:208). Every contrast check that uses the 3.0:1 threshold — LOW_ANSI_BLACK_CONTRAST (line 108), LOW_ANSI_BRIGHT_BLACK_CONTRAST (line 111), and LOW_SELECTION_CONTRAST (line 136) — is therefore *structurally incapable* of ever producing a FAIL. The pass/fail gate in both _doctor (cli.py:610: `exit_code = 1 if any(d.severity == 'fail' ...)`) and _print_remaining_failures (cli.py:1508: `blocking = [d for d in remaining if d.severity == 'fail']`) only counts `fail`, so these checks never affect the success determination. Result: a profile with the exact 'ANSI black contrast' failure mode the tool advertises detecting is reported as PASSING. Reproduction (dark bg #090C16, fg #E5EBF5, Ansi 0 #10131A, no light/dark drift): `doctor` emits `[warn] LOW_ANSI_BLACK_CONTRAST: ANSI black contrast is low (1.05:1 < 3.0:1)` yet returns EXIT CODE 0; `doctor --json` returns `"passed": true` with summary fail=0. A selection case (Selection #303030 / Selected Text #3A3A3A => 1.16:1) likewise returns passed:true. The bad-ansi-black.json fixture only yields exit 1 incidentally because it ALSO contains an ITERM_LIGHT_DARK_DRIFT fail; strip the drift and the 1.05:1 invisible ANSI black passes silently. The 3.0:1 threshold is checked and printed but rendered inert for the pass/fail decision, contradicting the README claim of detecting 'common glass-terminal readability failures'.
fix: Decouple severity from the WCAG-AA threshold. Pass an explicit severity (or `blocking: bool`) into _check_pair for each call site so ANSI-black/bright-black and selection contrast deficits below 3.0:1 are treated as blocking (FAIL), or alternatively make _print_remaining_failures / _doctor treat WARN-level contrast deficits as blocking. As-is, the only contrast checks that can ever block are the four 4.5:1 ones (foreground/bold/ansi7/ansi15); the 3.0:1 ones are decorative. Add a regression test that a profile failing only ANSI-black contrast (no drift) yields a nonzero doctor exit and is not reported as 'passes'.

verdict.reasoning: Verified against real code and reproduced empirically. diagnostics.py:208 sets severity via `FAIL if threshold >= 4.5 else WARN`. The three contrast checks using the 3.0 threshold — LOW_ANSI_BLACK_CONTRAST (line 108), LOW_ANSI_BRIGHT_BLACK_CONTRAST (line 111), LOW_SELECTION_CONTRAST (line 136) — therefore can never emit FAIL, only WARN. Both pass/fail gates count only `fail`: cli.py:610 (`exit_code = 1 if any(d.severity == 'fail' ...)`) and cli.py:1508 (`blocking = [d for d in remaining if d.severity == 'fail']`). Reproduced: a profile with Guid + Minimum Contrast set and Ansi 0 #10131A on bg #090C16 (1.05:1) produces only `warn LOW_ANSI_BLACK_CONTRAST`, `any fail: False`; CLI `doctor --json` returns `"passed": true`, `"fail": 0`, exit 0. README:14 advertises 'Static diagnostics for common glass-terminal readability failures', so an invisible-ANSI-black profile being reported as passing contradicts the stated purpose. The bad-ansi-black.json fixture's nonzero exit comes entirely from a confounding `fail ITERM_LIGHT_DARK_DRIFT`; the two ANSI-black diagnostics in it are both `warn`, confirming the claim that stripping the drift makes it pass silently. The only test (test_diagnostics.py:26) asserts the code is present, not that it blocks. isNew: the round-2 fix commit (9133840) did not address this; the threshold-coupled severity rule remains. Severity calibration note: this is an under-gating/missing-FAIL policy gap (no crash or data loss), and 3.0:1 is a legitimately lower-severity WCAG-large threshold, so a design could defensibly keep these as warnings — that pulls it toward the HIGH/MEDIUM boundary. But because the tool's headline diagnostics become structurally inert for its own pass/fail contract and a genuinely unreadable profile reports as passing, lower-edge HIGH is justified.

title: set_color/to_iterm_dict clobbers non-sRGB Color Space and drops extra dict keys on round-trip
description: from_iterm_dict() reads only the four numeric components and discards the 'Color Space' value and any other keys present in the source dict. to_iterm_dict() then hardcodes "Color Space": "sRGB". So any iTerm color stored in a wide-gamut space (e.g. "P3", "Calibrated", "Display P3") is silently reinterpreted as sRGB when the tool rewrites it, and the component values are kept verbatim while the space label changes — that is a visible color shift, not a no-op. Concrete repro: a profile whose Background Color is {"Color Space":"P3", R/G/B...}; after profile.set_color('Background Color', profile.color('Background Color')) (an idempotent re-set), the written document has "Color Space":"sRGB". Verified: orig 'P3' -> after set_color 'sRGB' (CHANGED: True). Any sibling keys iTerm may attach to a color dict are also lost. This affects presets.py (lines 177-179) and fixes.py (line 31) which write colors via to_iterm_dict, and iterm_profile.set_color (line 79).
fix: Preserve the original dict's Color Space (and any unknown keys) on rewrite. Either (a) have from_iterm_dict capture the source space and to_iterm_dict emit it, or (b) in ItermProfile.set_color, merge the new components into the existing dict rather than replacing it: start from a copy of the current value (if a dict) and overwrite only the four component keys, leaving Color Space and other keys intact. Components staying numerically identical while the space flips is the actual bug to close.

verdict.reasoning: Verified against real code and reproduced empirically. color.py:32-41 from_iterm_dict reads only the four numeric components; color.py:46-53 to_iterm_dict hardcodes "Color Space":"sRGB". The Color dataclass has no field for the color space or any other dict key, so both are unrecoverable on round-trip. iterm_profile.py:78-79 set_color does a full replace (self.profile[key] = color.to_iterm_dict()), not a merge. presets.py:177-179 and fixes.py:31 also write via to_iterm_dict. My repro with the venv interpreter confirmed: an idempotent profile.set_color('Background Color', profile.color('Background Color')) on a {"Color Space":"P3", ...} dict yields "Color Space":"sRGB" (CHANGED: True), drops a sibling key ("Some iTerm Extra Key" gone), and keeps the numeric components byte-identical — i.e. identical numbers reinterpreted under a different gamut, a genuine visible shift on wide-gamut displays. iTerm2's Dynamic Profile format does support non-sRGB spaces (e.g. P3), so this is a real data-fidelity bug, not theoretical. The proposed fix (merge new components into a copy of the existing dict, or capture/emit the space) is correct. Severity is appropriately MEDIUM: silent, user-facing, but scoped to the minority of profiles using wide-gamut spaces, with no crash, no broad data loss, and components themselves preserved.

title: from_iterm_dict silently defaults missing components to 0/1, so partial/corrupt color dicts are not flagged malformed
description: from_iterm_dict uses value.get('Red Component', 0.0) etc. A color dict missing Green/Blue (truncated or corrupt) parses as a valid Color with those channels forced to 0.0 instead of raising. Consequently ItermProfile.is_color_malformed() (iterm_profile.py:64-76) returns False for such a dict — it cannot distinguish 'complete color' from 'partial dict missing channels'. Round-trip counterexample: profile with Foreground Color = {"Red Component":0.5} (no Green/Blue). color() returns Color(0.5, 0.0, 0.0); is_color_malformed returns False; and to_iterm_dict then writes a fully-populated dict {R:0.5, G:0.0, B:0.0, A:1.0, Color Space:sRGB}, so reading back the written artifact yields different data than the original (a black-tinted red materialized from a partial record). The malformed-detection contract is silently violated.
fix: Treat absence of any of Red/Green/Blue Component as malformed: require the three RGB keys to be present (Alpha may default to 1.0), and raise ValueError when any is missing so is_color_malformed correctly reports True and partial dicts round-trip as corrupt rather than as a fabricated color.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against real code and reproduced via the project venv. color.py:32-41 `from_iterm_dict` uses `value.get("Red Component", 0.0)` / `get("Green Component", 0.0)` / `get("Blue Component", 0.0)`, so a partial dict like `{"Red Component": 0.5}` parses to Color(0.5, 0.0, 0.0, 1.0) without error. iterm_profile.py:64-76 `is_color_malformed` returns True only when `from_iterm_dict` raises; since missing RGB keys do NOT raise, it returns False for partial dicts. Reproduced exactly: `is_color_malformed` returned False, `color()` returned Color(0.5,0,0,1), and `to_iterm_dict()` materialized a fully-populated dict, so a truncated/corrupt color round-trips into a fabricated black-tinted red rather than being flagged. The contract concern is real and well-grounded: diagnostics.py:36-59 exists specifically to FAIL-flag present-but-unparseable color dicts so contrast checks aren't silently skipped; a partial dict defeats exactly that intent, fabricating a color and running contrast checks against invented channel values. All three real fixtures and iTerm2 exports always include R/G/B (plus Alpha and Color Space), so there is no legitimate partial-RGB shorthand — RGB absence genuinely signals corruption, while the Alpha default of 1.0 is acceptable. The proposed fix (require the three RGB keys, raise ValueError when any is missing) is correct and matches the existing error-to-malformed plumbing.

isNew: Not addressed in rounds 1-2. Commits af8a318 (26 audit findings) and 9133840 (19 round-2 findings) touched decoding/contrast/IO/watch and Otsu/timeouts, but the `.get(key, default)` pattern in from_iterm_dict is unchanged, and existing is_color_malformed tests cover only absent key, valid dict, and non-numeric components (string/list/dict) — none test a partial dict missing RGB channels. So this specific gap is genuinely new.

Severity: MEDIUM is appropriate, not higher. It requires an atypical malformed input (truncated or hand-edited dict missing RGB keys, never produced by iTerm2 itself), and the failure mode is a misleading/silently-skipped diagnostic plus a fabricated color in the written artifact — a soundness/correctness gap, not a crash, security issue, or corruption of valid input. Confidence high because the behavior was directly reproduced and the consumer (diagnostics malformed-detection contract) is concrete.

title: install-watch-daemon bakes config/CLI numeric values that config validation and watch-live both reject into the AutoLaunch command (no validation)
description: _install_watch_daemon resolves interval/stable/cooldown from CLI flags or the [daemon]/[watch] config but performs NO range validation before passing them to watch_live_command(), which simply string-formats them into the generated watch-live command line. config-check (config.py _validate_float/_validate_int) rejects interval<=0, stable<1, cooldown<0, and WatchLiveConfig.__post_init__ (watch_live.py:60-67) rejects the same at runtime. So the three layers disagree: the daemon installer is the only one that accepts these invalid values. Reproduction: `python -m term_chameleon.cli install-watch-daemon --dry-run --stable 0 --interval -1 --cooldown -5` succeeds and emits `Command: ... watch-live --yes --duration 315360000 --interval -1.0 --stable 0 --cooldown -5.0 ...`; the identical values in a [daemon] section fail config-check with `[daemon].interval: expected number > 0`, `[daemon].stable: expected integer >= 1`, `[daemon].cooldown: expected number >= 0`. The same holds when the bad values come from --config. The installed AutoLaunch script therefore launches a watch-live process that immediately raises ValueError on every iTerm2 startup, a silent permanently-broken daemon. region is likewise unvalidated: `install-watch-daemon --dry-run --region not,a,valid,region` bakes the garbage region into the command, while both config-check and `watch-live --region not,a,valid,region` reject it with `region values must be integers`.
fix: In _install_watch_daemon, validate the resolved daemon_interval/daemon_stable/daemon_cooldown and resolved_region with the same rules used by config validation / WatchLiveConfig before calling watch_live_command (e.g. construct a throwaway WatchLiveConfig, or call Region.parse on resolved_region, and raise ConfigError on failure). This makes the installer reject exactly what config-check and watch-live reject.

verdict.reasoning: Verified against real code and reproduced every assertion. _install_watch_daemon (cli.py:724-748) resolves daemon_interval/daemon_stable/daemon_cooldown and resolved_region from CLI flags or [daemon]/[watch] config, then passes them straight to watch_live_command (watch_daemon.py:111-150), which only str()-formats them into the watch-live command line — no range/format validation. Only initial_mode gets a guard (_preset_or_error). Reproduced: `install-watch-daemon --dry-run --stable 0 --interval -1 --cooldown -5` succeeds and emits `--interval -1.0 --stable 0 --cooldown -5.0`. The three other layers reject the identical values: (1) config-check on a [daemon] section with the same values fails with `[daemon].interval: expected number > 0`, `[daemon].stable: expected integer >= 1`, `[daemon].cooldown: expected number >= 0` (config.py _validate_float/_validate_int via _validate_watch_like_section:228-232); (2) `watch-live --yes --interval -1.0 ...` errors `interval must be > 0` (WatchLiveConfig.__post_init__ watch_live.py:59-69); (3) region: daemon bakes `--region not,a,valid,region` while both config-check and `watch-live --region not,a,valid,region` reject with region-integer errors. The live watch-live code path (cli.py:1138-1146) builds a WatchLiveConfig and calls Region.parse, so it validates; the daemon installer is the only entry point that skips both. The baked command would raise ValueError on every iTerm2 AutoLaunch, yielding a silent permanently-broken daemon. isNew: the current source at 724-748 contains no such validation, so this is not addressed by prior rounds. Severity adjusted to MEDIUM rather than HIGH: it is a genuine three-layer consistency/robustness defect, but it requires the user to deliberately supply invalid input to an opt-in daemon-install command (not attacker-controlled, no data loss, no security impact), and the failure mode is a non-functioning daemon that's still surfaced (the user explicitly passed the bad values). The proposed fix (construct a throwaway WatchLiveConfig and call Region.parse on the resolved values, raising ConfigError on failure) is correct and aligns the installer with the other two layers.

title: watch-live has no --whole-screen / --no-iterm-window flag, so config [watch] iterm_window=true cannot be overridden from the CLI (contradicts README 'explicit CLI flags override config values')
description: README line 97 states 'watch-live, install-watch-daemon, and setup accept --config; explicit CLI flags override config values.' For watch-live the --region/--iterm-window mutually-exclusive group has no --whole-screen member (install-watch-daemon DOES define one at cli.py:134-138). In _watch_live, when the user passes neither --region nor --iterm-window, resolved_iterm_window falls back to bool_value(value(watch_cfg,'iterm_window'), False). If config sets [watch] iterm_window=true, there is no CLI invocation that forces whole-screen sampling: omitting flags yields iterm_window=true (config wins), and there is no negative flag. Reproduction: with a config containing `[watch]\niterm_window = true`, running watch-live with no region/window flag resolves resolved_iterm_window=True with no way to get False short of passing an explicit --region. So the one watch flag that is a boolean is not CLI-overridable to its False state, unlike the daemon equivalent.
fix: Add a --whole-screen action='store_true' option to the watch-live region_group (mirroring install-watch-daemon) and thread it into _watch_live so that --whole-screen forces resolved_iterm_window=False and resolved_region=None, restoring full CLI override of the config iterm_window value.

verdict.reasoning: Verified against the real code and empirically reproduced. (1) Arg group: cli.py:288-292 defines watch-live's region_group with only --region and --iterm-window; no --whole-screen member. install-watch-daemon's group at cli.py:127-138 DOES include --whole-screen. (2) Resolution: in _watch_live (cli.py:1115-1121), when neither --region nor --iterm-window is passed, resolved_iterm_window = bool_value(value(watch_cfg,'iterm_window'), False). The daemon's _install path (cli.py:714-723) has an explicit `elif whole_screen: resolved_iterm_window=False; resolved_region=None` branch giving full override. (3) README line 97 verbatim: "watch-live, install-watch-daemon, and setup accept --config; explicit CLI flags override config values." (4) Empirical: with /tmp/tc_test_cfg.toml containing [watch] iterm_window=true, simulating the exact _watch_live logic yields resolved_iterm_window=True with no flags; the only way to get False is --region (a different sampling mode, not whole-screen). `watch-live --whole-screen` errors "unrecognized arguments"; `install-watch-daemon --whole-screen` is accepted. So the config [watch] iterm_window=true boolean cannot be overridden to its False (whole-screen) state from the watch-live CLI, contradicting the documented override guarantee and creating an asymmetry with the daemon. isNew: git history shows watch-live's region_group is unchanged since commit fa05c58; the round-1 (af8a318) and round-2 (9133840) audit-fix commits did not add --whole-screen to watch-live; the daemon's --whole-screen came from an unrelated commit (271a427). Severity MEDIUM is fair: it's a documented-contract/UX gap, not a crash or security issue; impact is limited to users who set iterm_window=true in config and want per-invocation whole-screen sampling without rewriting config or switching to a region. The proposed fix (mirror install-watch-daemon: add --whole-screen store_true to region_group, thread it through, force resolved_iterm_window=False and resolved_region=None) is correct and minimal.

title: loads_document crashes with AttributeError on non-dict top-level JSON
description: loads_document() calls json.loads(text) and immediately does document.get("Profiles") without verifying the parsed root is a dict. JSON whose top-level value is anything other than an object (a number, array, string, or null) makes document.get raise an uncaught AttributeError instead of a clean ValueError. This is reachable from the public file entrypoint load_profile() and thus from many CLI commands (status.py:61, fixes.py:75, modes.py:22, visual.py:89, deterministic_check.py:48, cli.py:601) that read user-supplied iTerm2 Dynamic Profile JSON files. Reproduction (all crash): write a profile file whose entire content is `5` -> AttributeError: 'int' object has no attribute 'get'; `[1,2,3]` -> AttributeError: 'list' object has no attribute 'get'; `"hello"` -> AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'get'; `null` -> AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'get'. Verified via load_profile() on a temp file: `from term_chameleon.iterm_profile import load_profile; load_profile(path_to_file_containing_5)` raises AttributeError. The fuzz harness output: `[file_toplevel_int] *** CRASH AttributeError: 'int' object has no attribute 'get'`.
fix: After document = json.loads(text), guard the type before accessing keys: `if not isinstance(document, dict): raise ValueError("iTerm2 Dynamic Profile JSON root must be an object")`. Place this immediately before the document.get("Profiles") call on line 98.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified against real code and reproduced end-to-end. iterm_profile.py:97-98 does `document = json.loads(text)` then `document.get("Profiles")` with no type guard on the root. All four reported inputs crash: writing a file containing `5`, `[1,2,3]`, `"hello"`, or `null` and calling load_profile() raises AttributeError ('int'/'list'/'str'/'NoneType' object has no attribute 'get') instead of a clean ValueError.

Reachability confirmed at the public CLI boundary: the `doctor` command dispatches via _doctor -> load_profile inside the try block at cli.py:409, but the only except (cli.py:595) catches (ValueError, OSError, JSONDecodeError, RuntimeError) — NOT AttributeError. Running cli.main(['doctor', <file containing 5>]) produces an UNCAUGHT AttributeError (verified directly), so the user gets a raw traceback instead of the intended `error: ...` message + exit code 2. The `fix` command shares the same boundary. (status.py:61 catches broad Exception, so that path degrades gracefully — but cli doctor/fix do not.)

isNew: Git history shows rounds 1-2 hardened this file (26 + 19 findings) and even added an inner `isinstance(profile, dict)` guard at line 107, but the root-level dict guard was never added — the `document.get("Profiles")` line has been unguarded since introduction. Not among the prior fixes.

Severity: I'd put this at MEDIUM rather than HIGH. It is a real uncaught-exception/crash on malformed user input (correctly a bug — code-review.md flags missing input validation at system boundaries), but the impact is a stack trace and nonzero exit on a hand-crafted invalid profile file, not data loss or security exposure. JSONDecodeError (genuine garbage) is already handled cleanly; this only affects syntactically valid JSON with a non-object root. The proposed fix (isinstance(document, dict) check raising ValueError before line 98) is correct and minimal.

title: read_png performs unbounded zlib decompression with no dimension or output cap (decompression-bomb amplification)
description: read_png() calls zlib.decompress(b"".join(idat_parts)) with no max_length, and IHDR width/height (read on lines 24-26) are used directly with no upper bound before allocating one Color object per pixel. A small, highly compressible IDAT expands enormously: a verified 46,675-byte PNG file (4000x4000, color type 2, all-zero scanlines) decompresses to ~48 MB of raw scanline data and allocates 16,000,000 Color objects, taking ~15.6 seconds and large memory on the test machine. Larger declared dimensions push this toward MemoryError / multi-minute hangs. Unlike image_stats() (images.py:106) which accepts a max_pixels cap, the PNG decode path has no equivalent guard. read_png is reachable from iterm_window.py:101 and screenshot_test.py:124 on captured/provided image files. This is a resource-exhaustion / DoS amplification vector rather than a hard crash. Reproduction: build the PNG with `zlib.compress(b'\x00' * (4000*(1+4000*3)))` as the IDAT and a matching IHDR, then call read_png(path); it returns 16M pixels after ~15s.
fix: Add a sanity cap on declared dimensions right after IHDR parsing (e.g. reject width*height above a configurable MAX_PIXELS with a clear ValueError), and pass a bounded max_length to zlib.decompress (decompressing in a loop and aborting once output exceeds the expected height*(1+width*channels) size, since the exact expected raw length is known from IHDR). This bounds both CPU and memory before any per-pixel allocation.

verdict.reasoning: Verified against the real source and reproduced empirically. png.py:45 calls zlib.decompress(b"".join(idat_parts)) with no max_length, and IHDR width/height (parsed at lines 24-26) are used directly at lines 48-66 to allocate one Color object per pixel with no upper bound. I built the exact PNG the claim describes (4000x4000, color type 2, all-zero scanlines): a 46,732-byte file decompressed to 48,004,000 raw scanline bytes and produced 16,000,000 Color objects in ~18.4s on the .venv interpreter — confirming the ~1000x amplification and multi-second hang. Declared dimensions can be pushed arbitrarily higher toward MemoryError. The max_pixels guard in image_stats (images.py:106-123) does NOT protect this path: the call flow analyze_image_file -> read_image_file -> read_png (screenshot_test.py:111-124) fully decodes and allocates all pixels in read_png BEFORE max_pixels sampling is ever applied, so the guard is purely cosmetic against this vector. read_png is reachable from iterm_window.py:101 and screenshot_test.py:124. Threat model caveat: there is no network/untrusted-upload boundary — read_png runs on locally captured screenshots and user-supplied baseline/comparison image paths in a CLI tool, so this is local resource-exhaustion hardening rather than a remote DoS, which keeps it at MEDIUM rather than HIGH. isNew: git history shows the only prior audit touch to png.py (af8a318) added an IHDR length-check and wrapped decompress in try/except for corruption, but added neither a max_length on decompress nor a dimension cap; this specific bomb/unbounded-allocation gap is unaddressed by rounds 1-2.

title: Contrast deficit messages can display the threshold value as the failing ratio due to 2-decimal rounding
description: format_ratio rounds to 2 decimals while the comparison uses the raw float (`ratio < threshold`). A ratio of 4.497 fails the 4.5 check but renders as `4.50:1`, producing the self-contradictory message `Foreground contrast is low (4.50:1 < 4.5:1)`. Likewise 2.996 renders `3.00:1 < 3.0:1`. Confirmed via format_ratio(4.497) => '4.50:1'. Narrow band and cosmetic only (does not affect the pass/fail logic), but the printed message looks wrong to a user reading the report.
fix: Either floor/truncate the displayed ratio toward the failing side (e.g. round down) or widen displayed precision when the rounded value equals the threshold, so the message never shows a displayed ratio >= the displayed threshold while still failing.

verdict.reasoning: Verified against actual source. contrast.py:24-25 defines `format_ratio(value) -> f"{value:.2f}:1"`, rounding the displayed ratio to 2 decimals. diagnostics.py:204-205 (_check_pair) compares the raw float: `ratio = contrast_ratio(...)` then `if ratio < threshold`. diagnostics.py:210 builds the message `f"{label} contrast is low ({format_ratio(ratio)} < {threshold:.1f}:1)"`. Reproduced empirically with the project venv: format_ratio(4.497) => '4.50:1' while 4.497 < 4.5 is True, and format_ratio(2.996) => '3.00:1' while 2.996 < 3.0 is True. The thresholds passed at diagnostics.py:102-142 are real (4.5 and 3.0), so the contradictory band is reachable: any ratio in [threshold-0.005, threshold) fails the check yet renders >= the displayed threshold, producing e.g. 'Foreground contrast is low (4.50:1 < 4.5:1)'. The band is exactly 0.005 wide per threshold — narrow but genuine. The defect is purely cosmetic: it does not affect pass/fail logic (comparison uses the raw float), so LOW severity is appropriate. isNew: round-1 commit af8a318 touched contrast.py, but git diff shows it only changed the translucent-background compositing (blend_over) logic, not format_ratio or the message formatting; no test references format_ratio or the rounding contradiction. Not fixed in rounds 1-2.

title: Inconsistent rounding between Color.to_hex (banker's rounding) and images._clamp_byte (round-half-up) for exact-half components
description: to_hex uses Python round() which is round-half-to-even, while images._clamp_byte (images.py:186-187) uses math.floor(value*255 + 0.5) which is round-half-up. For any component whose value*255 lands exactly on N.5, the two quantizers disagree. Verified counterexamples: Color(126.5/255,...) -> to_hex byte 126 but _clamp_byte 127; Color(0.5/255,...) -> to_hex 0 but PPM byte 1; Color(2.5/255,...) -> to_hex 2 but PPM byte 3. A single in-memory Color therefore serializes to a hex string and a PPM pixel that represent different 8-bit values, breaking the invariant that hex and PPM encode the same color. Impact is small because exact-half float components do not arise from byte-derived colors (n/255 never equals (k+0.5)/255), only from synthetic/gradient/blended colors.
fix: Use one shared quantizer for all 8-bit serialization. Replace round(self.r*255) in to_hex with the same _clamp_byte rounding (or vice versa) so hex and PPM are guaranteed to agree, and factor the quantization into a single helper in color.py imported by images.py.

verdict.reasoning:
The technical core is verified against real code. color.py:44 to_hex uses `round(self.r * 255)` (Python round = banker's/round-half-to-even). images.py:187 _clamp_byte uses `math.floor(value * 255 + 0.5)` (round-half-up). I reproduced all three claimed counterexamples with the project venv: for 126.5/255 to_hex byte=126 vs clamp=127; 0.5/255 -> 0 vs 1; 2.5/255 -> 2 vs 3. The disagreement is real and occurs precisely for components whose value*255 lands exactly on N.5.

The impact note is also accurate: byte-derived colors (n/255) can never equal (k+0.5)/255, so the mismatch only arises for synthetic/gradient/blended Colors, and only for the measure-zero set of exact-half floats.

However, the claim's framing of "breaking the invariant that hex and PPM encode the same color" overstates it. The two quantizers live on entirely separate output paths: to_hex feeds OSC escape sequences, the iTerm API, and contrast diagnostics; _clamp_byte/_rgb_bytes is used ONLY in images.py write_ppm. No code path requires a single Color to encode bit-identically across both hex and PPM, and nothing in the codebase compares them. So there is no actual relied-upon invariant being violated, and no observable user-facing failure. This is a genuine code-quality/consistency issue (two different 8-bit rounding conventions in one library), correctly rated LOW. The proposed fix (one shared quantizer helper) is reasonable and aligns with DRY.

isNew: confirmed not fixed in rounds 1-2 — _clamp_byte's floor-based rounding has been unchanged since introduction (commit c50a232) and the round-1/2 audit fix commit af8a318 did not touch this rounding logic; both code locations still exhibit the divergence in current HEAD.

title: color_hex() calls from_iterm_dict without guarding ValueError on out-of-[0,1] (wide-gamut) components
description: Color.__post_init__ rejects any component outside [0.0, 1.0]. iTerm wide-gamut (P3/extended-range) colors legitimately store components <0 or >1 (e.g. R=1.0931, G=-0.0227). color_hex() invokes Color.from_iterm_dict(value).to_hex() with no try/except, so it raises ValueError on such a profile dict instead of returning a clamped hex or None. Verified: color_hex({'Foreground Color': {'Red Component':1.0931,...}}, 'Foreground Color') raises 'r component must be between 0 and 1'. Unlike ItermProfile.color() (lines 50-62) which guards from_iterm_dict in try/except, color_hex is unprotected. Severity is LOW because color_hex currently has no production callers (only exercised in tests/test_extreme_stress.py), but it is a public function and a latent round-trip crash for real wide-gamut exports.
fix: Either clamp out-of-range components in from_iterm_dict before constructing Color (when the intent is sRGB output), or wrap the from_iterm_dict call in color_hex in try/except (ValueError, TypeError) and return None on failure, mirroring ItermProfile.color().

verdict.reasoning: Verified against the real code. Color.__post_init__ (color.py:13-16) raises ValueError for any component outside [0.0,1.0]. Color.from_iterm_dict (color.py:32-41) only catches TypeError, not the ValueError raised by __post_init__. color_hex (iterm_profile.py:121-125) calls Color.from_iterm_dict(value).to_hex() with no try/except, so an out-of-range component propagates a ValueError. ItermProfile.color() (lines 50-62) and is_color_malformed() (64-76) both guard from_iterm_dict in try/except (ValueError, TypeError), so color_hex is the asymmetric, unprotected sibling. Reproduced directly: color_hex({'Foreground Color': {'Red Component':1.0931,...}}, 'Foreground Color') raises 'r component must be between 0 and 1, got 1.0931'. The codebase has no wide-gamut/P3 handling (Color Space is hardcoded to sRGB on write; no read-side awareness), and iTerm2 does export P3 profiles with components outside [0,1], so the scenario is plausible. The claim is accurate that the only caller is tests/test_extreme_stress.py:487 (grep confirms no src/ callers), which correctly justifies LOW severity. The proposed fixes (clamp in from_iterm_dict, or wrap color_hex in try/except returning None like color()) are both valid. This is a genuine but minor inconsistency/latent crash in a public function with no production caller.

title: setup --name (and the 'X or config' pattern) lets config silently win over an explicit empty CLI value
description: _setup resolves name via `name or str_value(value(setup_cfg,'name'), 'Adaptive Glass Alpha')`. Because argparse default for --name is None and the resolution uses truthiness (`or`) rather than an explicit None check, an explicitly supplied empty string `--name ''` is falsy and is silently replaced by the config value (or the hardcoded default). Reproduction: with `[setup]\nname = "ConfigName"`, the resolution of an explicit empty --name yields 'ConfigName', so config wins over the explicit CLI value, contradicting the override claim for the empty-string edge. The same `X or default` falsy pattern is used for preset/initial_mode/output_dir, but those are constrained to choices or Path and cannot realistically be falsy, so name is the only practically reachable case.
fix: Use explicit `None` checks rather than truthiness for string flags that can legitimately be empty, e.g. `name if name is not None else str_value(value(setup_cfg,'name'), default)`. Alternatively document that --name '' is not a supported value. Low severity because an empty profile name has little practical use, but it is a genuine precedence inversion.

verdict.reasoning:
Verified at src/term_chameleon/cli.py:1032: `name=name or str_value(value(setup_cfg, "name"), "Adaptive Glass Alpha")`. The argparse default for `--name` is None (cli.py:249), and `str_value` returns its raw string unchanged when given a str (config.py:144-145). I reproduced the behavior with the venv: an explicit `--name ''` resolves to `'ConfigName'` when config has `name="ConfigName"`, and to `'Adaptive Glass Alpha'` with no config. So an explicitly supplied empty string is silently overridden by config/default — a genuine precedence inversion for the empty-string edge.

The inconsistency is confirmed by the two adjacent lines: `resolved_live` (cli.py:1025) and `resolved_region` (cli.py:713) both correctly use explicit `is None` checks with an inline comment ("Explicit CLI flag overrides config; None means unspecified"), proving the author knew the correct idiom but did not apply it to the string flag `name`. The claim's note that preset/initial_mode/output_dir are constrained (choices/Path) and not realistically falsy is accurate, leaving `name` as the only practically reachable case.

Severity is genuinely LOW (matching the claim): an empty profile name has no legitimate use, the fallback yields a sensible value rather than a crash or wrong result, and no realistic user input is affected. It is a correctness nit about override-precedence semantics, not a functional bug for normal use.

isNew: The `name or str_value` pattern has existed unchanged since commit 4885d12 (TOML config support) and was NOT touched by the round-1 fix (af8a318, 26 findings) or round-2 fix (9133840, 19 findings). Not among prior fixes.

title: E2EStageReport.passed property is dead code; deterministic-check re-derives visual pass independently (no masking, but a divergence risk)
description: The round-2 fix added E2EStageReport.passed (visual_checks_passed AND screenshot_captured is not False) and a correct CLI check in _e2e_stage. However, no programmatic aggregator consumes E2EStageReport.passed. The only non-CLI consumer, deterministic_check.run_deterministic_check (deterministic_check.py:62-85), ignores both e2e.passed and e2e.screenshot_captured and instead RE-PARSES e2e.visual_report_json to recompute visual failures. Today this is safe because deterministic_check always calls run_e2e_stage(capture=False) so screenshot_captured is None and both code paths agree on the visual-only verdict. But it is a latent divergence: the canonical pass/fail (the .passed property) and the deterministic aggregator now compute the same thing two different ways. If capture=True were ever wired into the deterministic path, the aggregator would silently drop a screenshot-capture failure — the exact masking class fixed in round-2 for the CLI. Verified: E2EStageReport.passed returns False for both a failed-capture and a failed-visual report (runnable: construct E2EStageReport(screenshot_captured=False, visual_checks_passed=True) -> .passed is False), yet grep shows no caller of .passed.
fix: Have deterministic_check's deterministic-e2e-stage step delegate to e2e.passed / e2e.visual_checks_failed instead of re-reading and re-parsing visual_report_json, so there is a single source of truth for the e2e verdict. Then the screenshot_captured term is automatically respected if capture is ever enabled.

verdict.reasoning: Verified every assertion against the real code. (1) E2EStageReport.passed exists at e2e_stage.py:26-31 and returns False for both a failed-capture report (screenshot_captured=False, visual_checks_passed=True) and a failed-visual report — confirmed by running the constructor through .venv python. (2) grep across src/ confirms no programmatic consumer of E2EStageReport.passed: the .passed references in deterministic_check.py and release_check.py belong to DeterministicCheckReport/CheckStep/pixel/text estimates, not E2EStageReport. The CLI _e2e_stage (cli.py:1330-1352) does NOT use .passed either — it independently re-checks visual_checks_passed and screenshot_captured. (3) deterministic_check.py:62-67 re-reads and re-parses e2e.visual_report_json to recompute visual_failures, ignoring both e2e.passed and e2e.screenshot_captured. (4) run_e2e_stage is hardcoded to capture=False in that only non-CLI caller (line 62), so screenshot_captured is None and both verdict paths agree today. The divergence is genuinely latent, not active masking: no API surface routes capture=True into the deterministic path. So this is a real DRY / single-source-of-truth concern plus future-risk, with zero present-day incorrect behavior. LOW is the correct severity; the proposed fix (delegate to e2e.passed/visual_checks_failed) is sound. isNew=true: the round-2 fix introduced .passed and the CLI check but did not touch deterministic_check's re-parsing logic, so this observation about the second, divergent verdict path is distinct from the round-2 finding.
